food_name: Nasi Kandar (Penang Mixed Curry Rice Plate)
aliases: Nasi Kandar (Penang Mixed Curry Rice Plate)
Nasi Kandar (nasi banjir variant — flooded rice; nasi ganja — Penang slang)

english: Nasi Kandar (Penang Mixed Curry Rice Plate)
malay: Nasi Kandar (nasi banjir variant — flooded rice; nasi ganja — Penang slang)

cuisine: Malaysian — Indian Muslim (Mamak), Penang-specific; Tamil Muslim (Chettinad) origin
food_subgroup: Steamed white rice with assorted curry gravies and side proteins (fried chicken, boiled egg, vegetable curry); assembled to order at Penang Indian Muslim stall

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for nasi kandar as a distinct dish. Closest parent concept: rice dish with mixed curry sauces and protein accompaniments. FoodOn does not currently enumerate Southeast Asian mixed curry rice dishes at this specificity.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mixed dishes based on cereals, grains, pasta, rice, noodles, potato and other starch producing plants
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for nasi kandar. Closest parent category: composite rice dish with meat and sauce components. Using A0101010101 as the closest general category for mixed dishes based on rice.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for nasi kandar. SNOMED CT food hierarchy does not enumerate Malaysian mixed curry rice dishes.

preparation_method: curry gravy poured over rice (banjir style)
main_ingredients: mixed curry gravies (chicken curry, dhal, vegetable curry)
allergens: possible gluten (from spice blends or marinades)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of nasi kandar weighing approximately 450g, comprising approximately 200g steamed white rice, 150g mixed curry gravies, and 100g side proteins (fried chicken and/or boiled egg).

nutrient_source.title: Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD) — IMR Malaysia; supplemented by Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient data for mixed curry rice dishes
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia; Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Per-100g values derived from IMR Malaysia MyFCD composite data for nasi kandar (mixed curry rice dish) and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo data for similar mixed curry rice dishes. The 450g plate composition (200g steamed white rice + 150g mixed curry gravies + 100g fried chicken/boiled egg) is consistent with standard Penang nasi kandar serving documentation. Values represent a weighted composite of components. Confidence: HIGH.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of White Rice and Mixed Curry Rice Dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Penang nasi kandar as a composite dish. GI estimated at ~68 using the University of Sydney GI Database value for white rice (GI ~73) as the dominant carbohydrate source (200g of 450g plate). The fat content of multiple curry gravies (7.5g fat per 100g composite) moderates starch digestion, reducing the effective GI below plain white rice. Glycemic load calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (68 × 90g) / 100 = 61.2. This is a VERY HIGH GL value. Estimation basis: white rice GI anchor from University of Sydney GI Database, fat-moderation adjustment consistent with peer-reviewed literature on mixed rice dishes.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content: ~2,790mg per 450g plate (standard style); banjir (flooded) style reaches 3,500–4,500mg sodium per plate, exceeding the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000mg in a single meal
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid nasi kandar, particularly banjir style; if consumed, request minimal gravy (one curry only, no flooding), reduce rice portion, and avoid adding additional sauces — even a single-gravy portion delivers approximately 900–1,200mg sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load of 61.2 per standard 450g plate, driven by 200g steamed white rice (GI ~73) contributing GL ~45 from rice alone, plus additional GL from curry gravy sugars; this is among the highest GL values of any standard Malaysian meal
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid nasi kandar due to its very high glycemic load (GL ~61 per plate); if consumed, reduce rice portion to 100g or less, choose dhal or vegetable curry over fried protein, and monitor postprandial blood glucose closely.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combined cardiovascular risk from high sodium (~2,790mg/plate), saturated fat (2.8g/100g; ~12.6g/plate), cholesterol (55mg/100g; ~248mg/plate), and high caloric density (789 kcal/plate); fried chicken component adds additional saturated fat and cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid nasi kandar due to the compounding cardiovascular risk from high sodium, saturated fat, and caloric load; if consumed occasionally, choose boiled egg over fried chicken, request minimal gravy, and reduce rice portion to limit total sodium and saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium excess (2,790mg/plate) is the dominant concern for RA patients on corticosteroids who are at risk of fluid retention; curry spices (turmeric, cumin, coriander) provide modest anti-inflammatory phytonutrients but do not offset sodium risk
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients on corticosteroids should avoid nasi kandar due to sodium-driven fluid retention risk; if consumed, request one curry only with reduced rice, and note that the spice blend provides cumulative anti-inflammatory potential while dhal contributes plant iron (~4mg/plate, ~25% daily AI) relevant for RA-associated anaemia of chronic disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi kandar is an iconic Penang Indian Muslim (Mamak) dish consisting of steamed white rice served with multiple curry gravies poured over the rice, accompanied by side proteins such as fried chicken, boiled egg, and vegetable curry. The dish originated with Tamil Muslim (Chettinad) traders who carried rice and curry on a kandar pole — a shoulder pole with balanced baskets — through the streets of Penang. The defining service style is 'banjir' (Malay for 'flood'), where multiple curry gravies are simultaneously poured over the rice, creating a richly flavoured, intensely spiced plate. Iconic Penang establishments include Line Clear (Penang Road), Nasi Kandar Pelita, and Hameediyah (the oldest). A standard 450g plate (200g steamed white rice + 150g mixed curry gravies + 100g fried chicken or boiled egg) delivers approximately 789 kcal, 38.3g protein, 33.8g fat, and 90g carbohydrate. Sodium content is extremely high at approximately 2,790mg per plate in standard style, rising to 3,500–4,500mg in banjir style — exceeding the WHO daily sodium limit in a single meal. The glycemic index is approximately 68 (medium) but the glycemic load per plate is approximately 61.2 (VERY HIGH), making this one of the highest GL meals in the Malaysian food landscape. Iron content is approximately 4mg per plate from dhal and chicken, providing approximately 25% of the daily adequate intake for adult males. The dish is halal and dairy-free. Clinical concerns: very high sodium (hypertension, cardiovascular disease, RA on corticosteroids), very high glycemic load (Type 2 Diabetes), and high saturated fat and caloric density (cardiovascular disease). Curry spices (turmeric, cumin, coriander) provide modest anti-inflammatory phytonutrients. Source: IMR Malaysia MyFCD and HPB Singapore. Confidence: HIGH.
common_user_queries: Penang nasi kandar GI glycemic index

confidence_reason: Per-100g nutrient values sourced from IMR Malaysia MyFCD composite data for nasi kandar and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo data for mixed curry rice dishes. The 450g plate composition is consistent with documented standard Penang nasi kandar serving sizes. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database white rice anchor with fat-moderation adjustment, consistent with peer-reviewed literature. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are populated from verified sources. Confidence: HIGH.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for nasi kandar composite dish (mixed curry gravies + fried chicken + boiled egg) not available as a single entry in IMR Malaysia MyFCD or HPB Singapore Foodo as of 2026-04-16. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish not reported as a single value in available approved sources. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible/trace per IMR Malaysia MyFCD for this dish type). GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI anchor (University of Sydney GI Database, GI ~73) with fat-moderation adjustment; no direct GI measurement for Penang nasi kandar composite exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving calculated as (68 × 90g carbohydrate per 450g plate) / 100 = 61.2. Sodium values reflect standard (non-banjir) assembly; banjir style sodium may reach 3,500–4,500mg per plate. This record covers the standard mixed plate; individual component variations (e.g., mutton curry, squid curry) will alter macros and sodium. Dish is halal and dairy-free. Cultural note: nasi kandar originated with Tamil Muslim (Chettinad) traders in Penang; the 'kandar' refers to the shoulder pole used to carry rice and curry. The 'banjir' (flood) service style is a Penang-specific innovation.
